divergence: the clock was never invented — appointments are 'morning-ish', trains leave when full, and 'on time' cannot be translated
story_angle: solemn obituary for a beloved institution nobody can remember the purpose of
headline: National Schedule Coordination Board Ends 'Morning-ish' Operations
deck: Federal agency leaves a legacy of indecision, miscommunication, and 47,000 pages of 'pending clarification.'
article:
The National Schedule Coordination Board (NSCB), an enigmatic federal agency with a tradition of rendering the concept of schedules even more nebulous, has quietly ceased operations. The final director, whose departure is noted as having occurred 'sometime after the last coffee urn was emptied,' leaves behind a legacy steeped in non-definition and bureaucratic inertia.

Founded in 1972 with the sole purpose of issuing binding definitions of the phrase 'when full' for trains and community meetings, the NSCB presided over the nation’s collective confusion for over five decades. Its archives, containing an astounding 47,000 pages of handwritten notes largely reading 'pending further clarification,' now stand as a testament to the agency's remarkable ability to delay clarity indefinitely.

Despite the agency's complete dissolution two weeks prior, an annual budget totaling $4.8 million continued to be allocated for 18 years after the last employee, Director Hudson Plank, stopped reporting to the building on account of an unforeseen 'morning-ish' coffee break that stretched well into what can only be described as 'overdue.'

Local resident Margaret Bumble remarked, 'I always appreciated their commitment to ambiguity. It made my mornings a lot more exciting not knowing if anything would ever happen.' The NSCB may have dissolved, but its legacy of bureaucratic fog will surely linger long into the future—right around the time that appointments might normally be made.
author: Patience Worthington, Senior Obituary Correspondent

oped.title: Morning-ish Was Our Last Bastion of Civilization
oped.author: Archibald Pendleforth, Emeritus Chair of Procrastination Studies at the Academy of Eternal Wait
oped.body: The National Schedule Coordination Board's closure marks a dark day for those of us who cherish civilized ambiguity. Its 47,000 pages of pending clarification represented not bureaucracy but wisdom, shielding us from the tyranny of exactitude in a world where trains sensibly depart only when sufficiently crowded. Critics who now demand rigid timetables forget how the old system protected the dignity of the perpetually tardy. I, for one, never once arrived anywhere on purpose, and the Board's vagueness allowed my frequent 'morning-ish' cancellations to retain an air of thoughtful deliberation rather than mere forgetfulness. Blaming the agency for societal confusion is absurd; the real culprits are those impatient souls whose hat brims obscure their view of leisurely priorities. The proposed replacement agency, limited to 'whenever-ish' rulings, promises even greater volumes of clarification, restoring proper order.

classifieds.text: Former NSCB deputy selling 47,000 pages of 'pending clarification' binders, complete with coffee rings and three decades of handwritten question marks; first buyer to show up morning-ish gets the whole indecisive pile.
Regional rail operator urgently hiring one person who can convince passengers a train is 'full' without using clocks; must tolerate eternal waiting and explain why 'on time' still has no translation.
Lost: my entire career's worth of meeting confirmations after the Board shuttered; finder may keep the indecision if they return the single page that actually said when anything starts.
Apology from the ex-chair: every appointment scheduled under the old regime is now officially 'morning-ish forever'; please stop calling, we have already left the building at an undetermined hour.

panels.caption: Bureaucrat stamps the final stack: 'Board ends morning-ish ops.'
Aide: When exactly do we close? Bureaucrat: Add it to the pending pile.
The 47,001st page reads: 'Reconvene morning-ish. Status: eternal.'

joke.setup: The National Schedule Coordination Board has ended all 'morning-ish' operations in 2026,
joke.punchline: so your performance review now starts whenever your manager feels personally attacked by your existence.
joke.text: The National Schedule Coordination Board has ended all 'morning-ish' operations in 2026, so your performance review now starts whenever your manager feels personally attacked by your existence.

sponsor_ads.headline: National Schedule Coordination Board Certified Full-Train Assurance
sponsor_ads.body: Now that morning-ish is officially over, our sensors guarantee departure the exact second capacity is reached—no stragglers, no explanations, no appeals.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Late passengers will be catalogued, not accommodated.

sponsor_ads.headline: Post-Board Appointment Recalibration Kits
sponsor_ads.body: Convert your old 'morning-ish' meetings into rigid daylight increments using our patented shadow-length rulers and collective sighs.
sponsor_ads.tagline: If the sun disagrees, the meeting is cancelled.

sponsor_ads.headline: Strict Coordination Board-Compliant Vague Time Replacement
sponsor_ads.body: Tired of illegal 'ish' language? Our new phrase packs replace every flexible term with mandatory sequences of bird calls and traffic-light cycles.

sponsor_ads.headline: Emergency Schedule Coordination Board Appeal Forms
sponsor_ads.body: Missed your newly precise slot because the world still runs on old habits? Purchase pre-approved denial letters for the tribunal.
sponsor_ads.tagline: Processing begins when the queue is full.

stories.headline: Freight Companies Replace Timetables With 'When Full' Load Thresholds
stories.deck: Executives calculate that abandoning the former board's guidelines will cut annual paperwork by exactly 47,000 pages.
stories.article:
Trucking firms across the Midwest announced new departure rules yesterday requiring trailers to fill completely before any movement, ending prior attempts to align loads with the dissolved National Schedule Coordination Board's vague windows. Dispatchers now post signs stating only 'morning-ish or later' and record each truck's exit by the number of sandwiches consumed by drivers waiting in the yard.

One Omaha terminal reported its first complete convoy left after four days when the final pallet finally arrived, prompting management to praise the system for eliminating all disputes over whether a shipment counted as delayed.

stories.headline: Jury Duty Summonses Now Cite 'Whenever The Pool Fills' As Reporting Time
stories.deck: Courthouse staff report zero no-shows since the new phrasing replaced all clock references on official notices.
stories.article:
Federal courts in the District of Columbia updated all jury summons language this week to instruct citizens to appear once the waiting room reaches capacity rather than at any fixed hour. Bailiffs confirmed the change prevents arguments over whether a prospective juror arrived during the correct 'morning-ish' interval.

One panel was seated after only six hours when enough citizens gathered, allowing the trial to begin without the usual motions about tardiness that had previously stalled proceedings for entire weeks.
